scenario: hey yeah i'm i'm hanging in there
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hi matthew how are you the medical assistant told me that you injured your shoulder"] hey yeah i'm i'm hanging in there
[Clinician asked: "what happened"] yeah so well when i was younger i used to be a little bit more active with my friends with the playing

[Clinician asked: "pick up football and so you know me being a little bit older now i thought i'll take back up with no problem well i did i tried and all the other guys were a little bit bigger than me so i got i got tackled a couple of times and it just"] i kinda landed on my shoulder a little bit weird one time and so like i got back into the game i was okay but yeah ever since then the past couple of days it's just been it's been really bothering me
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and so when exactly did this happen"] yeah i'd say maybe it was probably about last weekends so i'd say about like four days ago
[Clinician asked: "okay well i'm happy at age 67 you're out there playing tackle football so good good for you and was it your right shoulder or your left shoulder that you injured"] it was my right
[Clinician asked: "okay and are you right or left handed"] i'm right handed
[Clinician asked: "okay and have you had any numbing or tingling in your fingers at all"] no fingers have been okay

[Clinician asked: "okay and any problems with the strength of the arm at all are you able to grab a cup of coffee"] yeah i hand's doing okay i can grab yeah i'm just trying to avoid lifting anything yeah anything anything anything a little bit too heavy like i had a chipment command so i had a box in the driveway the other day and reaching down and picking up the box was a little tough
[Clinician asked: "okay and where exactly on your shoulder does it hurt"] yeah it's kinda on the on the back side right right around here
[Clinician asked: "mm-hmm okay alright and are you able to lift up your shoulder so that your shoulder touches your ear at all"] yeah no that's that's that's hurting a little bit
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and what have you taken for the pain"] just a little bit of tylenol trying to take it easy
[Clinician asked: "did that help at all"] it it kinda like kept it at the same spot that has been but it really did n't make it go down
[Clinician asked: "okay so are your symptoms any better since it happened or they about the same"] no they are about the same
[Clinician asked: "okay and any neck problems did you injure your neck when this happened"] no neck has been okay

[Clinician asked: "okay and any other joint problems like does your elbow hurt on that side or your wrist or is it just your shoulder"] nope the the rest of it's doing okay it's just the just the shoulder
[Clinician asked: "okay and any other orthopedic injuries in the past have you ever injured that shoulder before as far as you know"] nope nope that one that one's been fine
[Clinician asked: "okay and any any other surgeries or any other medical problems"] i mean i had a appendicitis so i had an appendectomy
[Clinician asked: "okay"] maybe like three four years ago but that's about it

[Clinician asked: "okay alright well i'm just gon na go ahead and do a quick physical exam i'm gon na be calling out my exam findings i'll let you know what that means so and last thing have you had any fever or chills since this happened at all"] a slight headache but no no no fever
[Clinician asked: "okay did you hit your head when you when this happened"] a little bit
[Clinician asked: "okay alright did you pass out"] nope nope stayed conscious throughout

[Clinician asked: "okay alright well looking at your vital signs here in the office you do n't have any fever your blood pressure looks quite good it's about one twenty two over seventy six your heart rate is appropriate at eighty two beats per minute and your oxygenation is ninety five percent on room air on your cer"] yeah that that sounds great just being able to take a little bit of something for the pain would would would be really appreciated
[Clinician asked: "okay do you think you need something stronger than meloxicam"] no i'll give that a shot
[Clinician asked: "okay"] and see how that goes
[Clinician asked: "okay any questions"] when can i get back out on the field
[Clinician asked: "well you ca n't play taggle football for a while you have to have this fracture healed"] okay

[Clinician asked: "and i would avoid doing it you know as we get older you know our bones get a little bit weaker so i would avoid the tackle football maybe flag football in the future okay"] yeah yeah that sounds good
[Clinician asked: "okay anything else"] no i think that's that's been great thank you so much
[Clinician asked: "okay you're welcome have a good day bye"] thanks
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right shoulder injury.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Matthew Allen is a 67-year-old male, right-hand-dominant, male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right shoulder injury. The patient sustained this injury 4 days ago, when he was tackled a couple of times playing pick up football and landed on his right shoulder. He states that he was able to return to the game, however he has had pain since that time. The patient locates his pain to the posterior aspect of his shoulder. He denies any numbness or tingling in his fingers. He states that the strength in his hand is okay; however, he has been trying to avoid lifting anything too heavy. Mr. Allen denies being able to touch his shoulder to his ear because of pain. The patient has been taking Tylenol for pain, which does not provide relief. He denies any neck problems or other joint problems. The patient denies any previous orthopedic injuries to his right shoulder. He denies any fever or chills since the injury, but does note a headache. The patient states that he hit his head at the time of the injury but did not pass out.

SURGICAL HISTORY

The patient has a history of an appendectomy 3 to 4 years ago.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever or chills.
Musculoskeletal: Reports right shoulder pain. Denies neck pain.
Neurological: Reports headache. Denies numbness or tingling.

VITALS

BP: 122/76.
HR: 82 bpm.
SpO2: 95% on room air.

PHYSICAL EXAM

NECK: Examination of the cervical spine reveals no tenderness to palpation. Full ROM.
HEAD: There does not appear to be any trauma. No ecchymosis or bruising.
MSK: Examination of the right shoulder reveals pain to palpation of the posterior shoulder at the acromioclavicular joint. There is decreased abduction and adduction to about 90 degrees only. Pain with supination and pronation of the right arm. Palpable radial artery pulse.

RESULTS

X-ray of the right shoulder reveals an acute acromioclavicular joint fracture.

ASSESSMENT

Right shoulder acute acromioclavicular joint fracture.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays revealed an acute acromioclavicular joint fracture. The plan is to place the patient in a sling and prescribe meloxicam 15 mg once a day to treat his pain. I also recommend that we obtain a CT scan of the right shoulder to evaluate further. I recommend that the patient attend formal physical therapy to strengthen his right shoulder in hopes of avoiding surgery in the future. In the meantime, I have advised him to avoid tackle football.

naive.response_text: I understand how frustrating it must be to deal with this shoulder injury, especially when you were looking forward to getting back into the game. It’s great that you’re being proactive about your recovery. Based on what we discussed, we’ll take a conservative approach to let the shoulder heal properly. This means avoiding any heavy lifting or activities that strain your shoulder for now. I’ll schedule a follow-up in a couple of weeks to monitor your progress and adjust your treatment plan as needed. In the meantime, make sure to rest and take the prescribed medication as directed. Remember, taking it easy now will help you get back to your active lifestyle sooner. You’ve got this, Matthew. Keep up the good work, and let’s get you back on the field soon!

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thanks for sharing all that with me, Matthew. It makes sense you're feeling frustrated and concerned about your shoulder injury, especially since it's keeping you from getting back out there playing football. I can tell how much that activity means to you, and I want to work together to get you back to doing what you enjoy.

From what you've described, it sounds like you might have a shoulder sprain or even a small fracture, given the mechanism of injury and the persistent pain. The first step will likely be getting some imaging, like an X-ray, to check the shoulder more thoroughly [PMID ?]. In the meantime, we'll focus on managing the pain and avoiding activities that strain your shoulder, like heavy lifting or playing tackle football for now. I know that's tough, but it's important to let your shoulder heal properly.

We'll also plan some gentle physical therapy exercises to keep your shoulder moving without causing more harm. For pain management, we'll continue with the meloxicam for now, but if that doesn't help, we can explore other options. I want to make sure you're as comfortable as possible while we figure this out.

Before you leave, could you tell me in your own words what you think we've agreed on? That way I can make sure everything is clear. And let's plan to check in in about a week to see how things are progressing. If you have any sharp pain or if the pain starts getting worse, don't hesitate to reach out sooner.
